title: BPTT: Training Sequences
concept: Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) is the algorithm used to train recurrent networks, extending standard backprop across unrolled steps.

exercises.prompt: Which type of neural network is Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) primarily used to train?
exercises.explain: BPTT is designed specifically for recurrent neural networks (RNNs) because they process sequential data step-by-step.

cards.title: Time-Traveling Math
cards.body: When you read a sentence, your brain connects the meaning of the last word to the first word. But how does an AI learn from sequences where the past affects the future? It uses a clever time-traveling math trick!

cards.title: What is BPTT?
cards.body: Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) is the specialized algorithm used to train Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs). Because RNNs process sequences step-by-step, BPTT 'unrolls' the network over time and runs standard backpropagation backward through the entire sequence of steps to update the weights.

cards.title: The Domino Effect
cards.body: Imagine a long row of falling dominoes. If the last domino falls in the wrong direction, you have to trace the fall backward, domino by domino, to find out where the push went wrong. BPTT is like tracing that mistake backward through time to adjust how each domino was set up.

cards.title: Predicting the Next Word
cards.body: Suppose you are training an RNN to predict the next word in the sentence 'I love robots'. At step 3, the network predicts 'pizza' instead of 'robots'. BPTT calculates the error at step 3, and then sends that error backward through step 2 ('love') and step 1 ('I') to fix the network's memory.

cards.title: Myth: Different Weights for Every Step
cards.body: Many people think BPTT uses a completely different set of weights for every single time step. In reality, the weights are shared across all time steps. BPTT updates these same shared weights by accumulating the changes needed at each step.

cards.title: Standard Backprop vs. BPTT
cards.body: Let's compare standard feedforward backpropagation with Backpropagation Through Time.

left.text: Sends errors back through layers of a static network in one single step.

right.text: Sends errors back through both layers and sequential time steps (unrolled time).

exercises.prompt: Why do we need Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) instead of just standard backpropagation for recurrent networks?

options.text: Standard backprop cannot calculate any gradients.
Standard backprop does not handle sequential dependencies over time steps.
Standard backprop is too fast for RNNs.
Standard backprop only works on images, not text.

exercises.explain: Standard backpropagation only works on static data with a single forward pass. Because RNNs have loops and process sequences over time, BPTT is needed to trace errors back through the temporal steps.

exercises.prompt: True or False: BPTT creates and trains completely unique weights for every individual time step in the sequence.
exercises.explain: In BPTT, the weights are shared across all time steps of the RNN, and the gradient updates are accumulated and applied to these shared weights.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how BPTT visualizes the sequence.
exercises.sentence: Before calculating gradients, BPTT ___ the recurrent network across the sequence of time steps.
exercises.explain: BPTT works by 'unrolling' the recurrent network into a sequence of steps, making it look like a deep feedforward network so gradients can flow backward.

exercises.prompt: Which of these describes an AI system correctly utilizing BPTT?

options.text: An AI that treats each word in a sentence as a separate, independent task with unique weights.
An AI that unrolls the network over a sentence, calculates the final error, and sends updates backward through the words.
An AI that only feeds text forward and never updates its parameters based on past mistakes.

exercises.explain: Option A incorrectly claims weights are different at each step, and Option C claims no feedback is sent backward. Option B correctly explains that BPTT sends error signals back through the time steps to update the shared weights.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following best describes how Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) trains an RNN?

options.text: It trains each time step as an independent network with different weights.
It only passes information forward and never updates the early time steps.
It unrolls the network over time and propagates error gradients backward through all steps.
It processes the sequence in reverse order starting from the output to the input.

exercises.explain: BPTT works by unrolling the recurrent network over time steps, calculating the loss at the end (or along the way), and then propagating the error gradients backward from the final step all the way to the first step to update the shared weights.
